site.name: Gjallr
site.tagline: Rescue dead WordPress sites. Rebuild them properly.
site.description: Gjallr pulls content, comments, URLs, media evidence, and SEO context out of WordPress wreckage, then serves it from a clean Laravel runtime.

hero.title: Rescue dead WordPress sites. Rebuild them properly.
hero.body: Gjallr extracts the useful ore from a broken WordPress install: content, comments, URLs, menus, media records, SEO fields, and enough archive evidence to rebuild with intent instead of panic.

sections.title: The useful parts survive the fire.
sections.body: Gjallr keeps the material a builder actually needs, without dragging WordPress along for the ride.

items.title: Content and structure
items.body: Posts, pages, taxonomies, menus, comments, nested comments, authors, slugs, and dates.

items.title: URLs and redirects
items.body: Legacy paths, query redirects, canonical hints, and enough routing evidence to avoid breaking old links.

items.title: Media evidence
items.body: Upload records, local file checks, recovered archive copies, and clear misses when a file is gone.

items.title: SEO context
items.body: Titles, descriptions, open graph fields, and the old clues that explain why a page mattered.

sections.title: Archive work is evidence, not magic.
sections.body: Internet Archive requests stay opt-in, bounded, and dry-run friendly. Gjallr can check availability, recover missing uploads, and suggest archived URLs. It does not pretend the archive is truth carved into stone.

items.title: Bounded by default
items.body: Limits are explicit, user agents are required for real requests, and dry runs come first.

items.title: Media recovery
items.body: Missing uploads can be recovered into Gjallr-owned storage without overwriting local evidence by accident.

items.title: URL discovery
items.body: Archived HTML paths become suggestions. A builder still decides what belongs in the revived site.

sections.title: Not a WordPress-shaped shadow.
sections.body: Gjallr is not here to keep the old machine coughing. It extracts the salvage, then gets out of the way.

items.title: No CMS cosplay
items.body: The rescued runtime is read-only. Editing belongs in the rebuild, not in the salvage rig.

items.title: No plugin theater
items.body: Themes and plugins are evidence. They are not reanimated.

items.title: No finished-site illusion
items.body: The build command writes dossiers. Humans and AI can use them to rebuild properly.

pipeline.title: From source mess to rebuild dossier.

steps.title: Analyze
steps.body: Profile the SQL dump and site tree. Find WordPress versions, URLs, plugins, themes, uploads, and archive candidates.

steps.title: Import
steps.body: Map posts, pages, comments, media, terms, menus, and redirects into clean Laravel tables.

steps.title: Serve
steps.body: Browse the rescued content through normal Laravel routes. No WordPress boot. No plugin roulette.

steps.title: Recover
steps.body: Ask Wayback for missing media and URL evidence when the archive is useful and the limits are clear.

steps.title: Build
steps.body: Write reconstruction dossiers with inventories, paths, media status, redirects, and builder notes.

boundary.title: Keep the source mess out of the runtime.
boundary.body: WordPress knowledge belongs in ingestion. Archive mechanics belong in Wayback. Runtime models, controllers, routes, and views stay clean. If `wp_*` leaks into the Laravel runtime, something has gone off the rails.

nameStory.title: A short alarm, not a mythology essay.
nameStory.body: Gjallr nods at Gjallarhorn, the horn sounded to wake gods and announce the end of an age. That fits because a dead WordPress site is already the end of an age. Gjallr sounds the alarm, gathers what survived, and turns wreckage into evidence for a proper rebuild.

cta.title: Start with the evidence.
cta.body: The repo has the commands, tests, and boundaries. The revived site is your job. Gjallr just makes the rubble legible.

footer.text: Built as a static project site. No Laravel runtime. No Jekyll. No ceremony.
